exemplar.title: How does the use of organic and chemical fertilisers impact soil Ph and Nitrate levels of Fenugreek seeds?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the use of organic and chemical fertilisers impact soil Ph and Nitrate levels of Fenugreek seeds?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Comprehensive table of contents aids navigation
Consistent use of key ESS terminology enhances technical communication
Overall report structure follows IA conventions
weaknesses: Formatting glitches (errant math symbols, page breaks) interrupt reading flow
Citation formatting is inconsistent and some references are incomplete
Redundancies and occasional leaps in logic impede seamless coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of 2 for structure, terminology and logic indicate mainly logical organisation with lapses in presentation and citation consistency. A mark of 2/3 reflects generally clear communication balanced against formatting and coherence weaknesses.

strengths: The research question is coherent, narrowly defined and specifies both dependent variables and the study organism
The environmental context is well discussed with citations and concrete examples (eutrophication, soil acidification)
Clear linkage between sustainable agriculture and environmental harm demonstrates relevance
weaknesses: Minor inconsistencies in wording and formatting (capitalisation of âpHâ) slightly reduce professionalism
Context section would benefit from subheadings and more precise citation formatting
Connections could be deeper with quantitative context or policy relevance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Based on strand scores of 5, 5 and 4 (average ~4.7), the student shows strong performance in identifying context but has minor clarity and depth omissions. To reflect both high quality and the need to balance the overall total of 15, a mark of 4/6 is awarded, acknowledging excellence in research question formulation and context while reserving full marks for more exhaustive linkage and flawless presentation.

strengths: Method outline is repeatable with treatment groups and trial structure
Justification of seed and fertiliser selection shows clear reasoning
Risk and ethical considerations are outlined with a variety of hazards addressed
weaknesses: Key variables (soil composition, temperature, light) are not fully specified, reducing method sufficiency
Sampling strategy is unclear with contradictory pot counts and no rationale for replicate numbers
Disposal protocols for excess fertilisers are not detailed, limiting risk assessment completeness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments yielded 3 (method), 1 (sampling) and 3 (risk), averaging ~2.3. The design is repeatable but insufficiently detailed and sampling is poorly justified. Awarding 2/6 reflects a midâlow band performance in planning while conserving marks for stronger areas.

strengths: Discussion links findings to broader issues in sustainable agriculture and EU policy
Strengths and weaknesses of the method are described, including trial replication and measurement limitations
Contextual evaluation connects results to realâworld agricultural practice
weaknesses: Improvement suggestions (e.g., pH meter) lack implementation details such as calibration procedures
Method evaluation does not fully explore precision, reliability or confounding factors
Further research extensions are generic without timelines or expanded variables
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand scores of 3 (method evaluation), 3 (conclusion evaluation) and 2 (improvements) average ~2.7, indicating a mid-band performance. A mark of 2/6 reflects that the student describes evaluation and improvements but does not consistently reach thorough, significant analysis and extension planning.

strengths: Clear t-test definitions and application aid reader understanding of statistical methods
Verbal interpretation of graphical trends is effective in highlighting treatment effects
Conclusion succinctly links numerical findings to environmental implications
weaknesses: Data tables duplicate entries and use inconsistent decimal notation, obscuring clarity
Measures of variability (standard deviations, error bars) are missing, limiting robustness
Statistical analysis omits control comparisons and detailed degrees-of-freedom reporting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With strand scores of 3 (presentation), 3 (analysis) and 4 (conclusion), the studentâs results and analysis are correct but incomplete. A 3/6 reflects appropriate data handling and valid conclusions while acknowledging shortcomings in data summarisation and statistical completeness.

strengths: Proposes precision agriculture solution showing real-world applicability
Balanced discussion of cost barriers for smallholders demonstrates nuanced evaluation
weaknesses: Connection between IA findings and the application is implied rather than explicitly justified
Environmental trade-offs (e-waste, energy use) and scalability details are not explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With strand assessments at 2 for both proposal and evaluation, the application of findings is described with moderate justification. A score of 2/3 acknowledges a thoughtful solution and some evaluation while noting the need for deeper justification and environmental considerations.

strandSelections.reasoning: Appropriate ESS terms (eutrophication, volatilisation, precision agriculture) are used, but inconsistent capitalisation and redundant phrasing reduce concision.  Either good terminology or concision is achieved, but not both consistently â 2.

The student relates findings to broader issues (soil health in the Netherlands, EU policy) and discusses how organic fertilisers mitigate environmental harm.  However, evaluation is descriptive and does not weigh contradictory evidence or quantify impacts, so it fits the 3-4 band at 3.

The conclusion directly states that synthetic fertiliser lowers pH and raises nitrate more than organic, referencing numerical differences and statistical significance.  It is largely supported by data, though it generalises beyond the four-week period.  Support is strong enough for the higher end (4) of the 3-4 band.

A two-sample t-test is carried out for each variable with correct formulas and interpretation; patterns in graphs are described.  Nevertheless, only Org vs Syn is tested, standard deviations are not shown, and control data are ignored.  Analysis is correct but incomplete â 3.

Improvements are limited to using a pH meter and acknowledging pot size; no concrete plan, timeline or suggestion for additional variables is presented.  This is more than superficial (two meaningful points) but not âsignificantâ, warranting the top of the 1-2 band (2).

A repeatable outline is presented (three treatment groups, three trials, weekly measurements).  However, masses, volumes and concentrations are partly ambiguous, mathematical symbols intrude, and there is no specification of controlled variables such as temperature or soil composition beyond mass.  The method is therefore only âappropriate but insufficientâ, fitting the 3-4 band at 3.

The student links the research to the issue by explaining how pH decline and nitrate runoff from fertilisers contribute to eutrophication and soil degradation, and by arguing that comparing organic vs synthetic inputs is relevant to sustainable agriculture.  Connections are described rather than fully dissected; quantitative links or policy relevance are missing, so the work reaches the top of the 3-4 band, not the comprehensive 5-6.

The student states a clear, narrowly-defined question (âHow does the use of organic and chemical fertilisers impact soil pH and nitrate levels of fenugreek seeds?â).  It specifies the two key dependent variables (pH, NO3-) and the organism under study, so it is both coherent and properly focused.  Minor wording inconsistencies (capitalisation, spelling of pH) do not detract from focus, justifying a band 5 rather than 6.

The student evaluates benefits (resource efficiency) and weaknesses (high cost for smallholders) of precision agriculture, covering more than one facet but omitting environmental trade-offs or scalability detail.  This meets the âsome relevant strengths and weaknessesâ descriptor at 2.

The report follows the prescribed IA sections and includes a contents page, but formatting glitches (math symbols, page breaks) interrupt flow.  Structure is present yet not sustained flawlessly, so 2 marks.

Strengths (multiple trials, dual measurements) and limitations (small pots, approximate strips) are described; some controls are mentioned.  The discussion lacks depth on reliability, precision or confounding factors, so it remains mid-band (3).

The environmental issue section discusses eutrophication, soil acidification, greenhouseâgas release and biodiversity loss caused by synthetic nitrogen fertilisers.  Specific examples (Pacific countries, UNEP 2021) and citations are provided, demonstrating a solid discussion that gives genuine context to the question.  Depth is good but not exhaustive, meriting the lower end (5) of the top band.

Sampling strategy is merely implied (three pots per treatment) and labelled âtrialsâ, with no explanation of why three replicates were chosen, how pots were allocated or how seeds were randomised.  Errors such as stating 9 pots but listing 3Ã3 treatments further undermine clarity.  This constitutes only an outline with omissions, placing it at the bottom of the 1-2 band (score 1).

The student lists gloves, ventilation, proper disposal and risks to local fauna/flora, demonstrating an organised outline of hazards and ethics.  Details (e.g., concentration thresholds, disposal protocols) are not comprehensive, but coverage is broader than a simple list, so a mid-band (3) is justified.

Tables include all recorded values and two graphs visually summarise trends.  Axes, units and legends are present, but duplication of rows and unconventional commas for decimals reveal omissions.  Thus diagrams are appropriate yet imperfect, earning 3.

Precision agriculture is proposed with explanation of how GPS and sensors reduce fertiliser over-use.  The link to the IA findings (need to limit synthetic inputs) is implied but not explicitly drawn; justification is moderate, so band 2 (2 marks) is appropriate.

Arguments generally proceed logically from data to implication, yet repetition of rows, symbol errors and occasional leaps (e.g., pot size to sustainability) impede reader comprehension at points.  This aligns with âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, earning 2.

annotations.comment: Student formulates a coherent and focused research question specifying dependent variables and organism, demonstrating strong clarity in scope.
Ensure consistent notation of pH (lowercase 'p' before 'H') and correct spelling to meet conventions and improve professionalism.
Use of a comprehensive table of contents aids navigation and demonstrates good organization, enhancing readability.
Paragraph broadly outlines eutrophication and biodiversity impacts but would benefit from concise subheadings and clearer citation formatting to strengthen context clarity.
Include quantitative figures (e.g., pH decline rates) when discussing soil acidification to provide stronger evidence and context.
Effective explanation of relevance connecting sustainable agriculture with environmental harm demonstrates solid linkage between issue and research.
Provide a citation for the Neem Cake description to support claims and ensure academic rigor in justifying method choice.
Math symbols in materials list appear unnecessary and may confuse readers; simplify by removing extraneous notation for clarity.
Specify how environmental conditions (e.g., light intensity, temperature, soil composition) are controlled to ensure repeatability and validity of method.
Material list shows 'â9 pots' which appears incorrect; clarify the intended number of pots to resolve sampling strategy ambiguity.
Justification of seed and fertilizer selection shows clear reasoning for organism and treatments, supporting methodological design purposefully.
Improvement suggestion to use a pH meter is valid but lacks detail on model, calibration procedure, and integration into methodology to be more meaningful.
Define specific disposal protocols for excess fertilizers (e.g., collection methods, safe storage locations) to complete the environmental risk assessment.
Table duplicates entries for week 1â3 and uses commas for decimals contrary to standard notation; consolidate replicates and use consistent decimal points.
Include measures of variability (e.g., standard deviation) alongside mean values in data tables to support the statistical analysis completeness.
Table 1 lists raw data without summarizing mean and variability, making it difficult to assess overall trends; consider including summary statistics.
Table 2 replicates rows without averaging replicates, leading to redundancy; present averaged data for clearer interpretation.
Graph clearly shows pH trends but would benefit from error bars to display data variability and enhance the robustness of analysis.
Including the calculated degrees of freedom and critical t-value in the statistical calculations would clarify the t-test application and support validity.
Clarify terminology in describing t-test; replace 'chance that the results are random' with probability of Type I error to demonstrate accurate statistical understanding.
Explicit definition of variables in the t-test equation helps readers follow the analysis and illustrates strong communication of statistical methods.
Effective verbal interpretation of graphical trends clearly distinguishes treatment effects, enhancing comprehension of data significance.
Consider performing statistical comparisons including the control group to fully contextualize treatment effects rather than focusing solely on Org F vs Syn F.
Conclusion succinctly summarizes findings and links them directly to environmental impact, reflecting solid integration of data and ecological implications.
Qualify conclusions by specifying the four-week study timeframe to avoid overgeneralization and accurately reflect the experimental limitations.
Demonstrates strong contextual evaluation by connecting findings to sustainable agriculture practices in the Netherlands, enhancing relevance.
Expand discussion of method limitations by considering potential confounding factors such as soil heterogeneity and measurement precision to deepen evaluation.
Explicit description of controlled variables (light, watering, soil type) highlights attention to experimental validity and reliability.
Proposing precision agriculture as a solution shows creative application of findings to real-world context, linking technology use to fertilizer management.
Strengthen the application by explicitly tying IA results (lower N runoff in Org F) to how precision tech can optimize organic fertilizer use based on sensor data.
Including considerations of cost barriers for smallholders acknowledges key limitation of proposed precision agriculture solution, demonstrating balanced evaluation.
Further evaluate environmental trade-offs of precision agriculture (e.g., electronic waste) and discuss scalability to strengthen the solution assessment.
Citations show variety of sources but formatting is inconsistent; adopt a single citation style (e.g., APA) to improve credibility and uniformity.
Entry 'USDA.' lacks full reference details (title, date); ensure all citations include complete information to avoid ambiguity.
